evidence: The statement asks for A ⊂ ℕ whose additive representation function 1_A∗1_A(n)/log n converges to a nonzero limit; erdosproblems.com/66 tags the problem 'additive basis'.
The docstring titles the statement 'The Erdős discrepancy problem' (unbounded discrepancy of ±1 sequences along homogeneous APs), proved by Tao [Ta16].
Docstring: 'The Erdős discrepancy problem (complex variant)' — the unit-circle-valued form of the same problem, also proved in Tao [Ta16].
The problem asks whether the product of an arithmetic progression n, n+d, ..., n+(k-1)d of length ≥ 4 with (n,d)=1 can be a perfect power.
The conjectured conclusion is that the product ∏ i ∈ s, i is never a perfect power q^l with l > 1.
Euler's case (k=4, l=2) of the same statement that a product of an arithmetic progression is not a perfect power.
Obláth's cases [Ob51] of the same statement that a product of an arithmetic progression is not a perfect power.
The statement is about M(n,k), 'the least common multiple of the finite set {n+1, ..., n+k}' (lcmInterval), and when two such values can coincide.
The statement compares M(n,k), the least common multiple of {n+1, ..., n+k}, across interval lengths; resolved by Cambie [Ca24] 'Resolution of an Erdős' problem on least common multiples'.
The problem asks whether the series ∑ 1/(n!-1) is irrational (Irrational in the Lean statement); erdosproblems.com/68 tags it 'irrationality'.
The series in question is ∑_{n≥2} 1/(n!-1), a sum over reciprocals of shifted factorials.
The statement is about p(m), 'the least prime factor of m' (Nat.minFac), asking whether p(n+k) > k^2+1 for some k for all large n.
Part (ii) asks whether the least-prime-factor bound fails when k^2+1 is replaced by e^{(1+ε)√k}+C_ε.
The statement asks whether for all large n there is k with n+k composite and least prime factor p(n+k) > k^2 (IsLPF in the file).
The statement explicitly requires n+k to be composite ((n + k).Composite) before bounding its least prime factor.
P(n,k) is defined as 'the largest prime factor of binom(n,k)' and the conjecture bounds it below by min(n-k+1, k^{1+c}).
The quantity bounded is the largest prime factor P(n,k) = (n.choose k).primeFactors.sup id of a binomial coefficient.
Sylvester and Schur [Er34] proved P(n,k) > k for k ≤ n/2, where P(n,k) is the largest prime factor of binom(n,k).
Erdős [Er55d] improved the lower bound on the largest prime factor of binom(n,k) to ≫ k log k for k ≤ n/2.
Heuristic variant: P(n,k) > e^{c√k} for the largest prime factor of binom(n,k), k ≤ n/2.
